title: An Anatomy of Crypto-Enabled Cybercrimes
abstract: The advent of cryptocurrencies and digital assets holds the promise of improving financial systems by offering cheap, quick, and secure transfer of value. However, it also opens up new payment channels for cybercrimes. Assembling a diverse set of public on- and off-chain, proprietary, and hand-collected data, including attacker–victim negotiations and dark web conversations in Russian, we present an initial anatomy of crypto-enabled cybercrimes, highlighting relevant economic issues and proposing areas for future research. Among others, we find ransomware, as the most dominant organized crypto-enabled cybercrime, entails criminal gangs that operate like firms who adopt modern revenue models and carefully manage their reputations. We suggest that blanket restrictions on cryptocurrency usage may prove counterproductive. Instead, blockchain transparency enables effective forensics for tracking, monitoring, and shutting down dominant cybercriminal organizations, which potentially facilitates a more secure and reliable crypto ecosystem in the longer term.

author.Will Cong: S.C. Johnson College of Business, Cornell University, Ithaca, New York 14853; and International School of Finance, Fudan University, Shanghai 200437, China; and National Bureau of Economic Research, Cambridge, Massachusetts 02138
author.Campbell Harvey: National Bureau of Economic Research, Cambridge, Massachusetts 02138; and Fuqua School of Business, Duke University, Durham, North Carolina 27708
author.Daniel Rabetti: National University of Singapore Business School, Singapore, Singapore 119240
author.Zong-Yu Wu: Palo Alto Networks, London EC4V, United Kingdom

article.1. Overview: Whereas decentralized finance has spurred innovations that hold the promise of improving our financial system and other industries, cybercriminals’ utilization of cryptocurrency has become a central focus of the regulatory debate, especially concerning ransomware attacks.1A recent report suggests that 73% of companies have experienced a ransomware attack in the last two years, almost 60% paid a ransom to restore their data, and 80% of those who paid a ransom demand were hit by ransomware a second time.2The cost of these attacks is not simply measured in dollars (more than $30 billion in 2023),3but also lives.McGlave et al. (2023)show that mortality rates increase by 0.77% in attacked hospitals as well, representing a 21% increase in deaths. Although cybercrimes are on the rise, attacked firms rarely disclose security breaches (e.g.,Amir et al. 2018,Chen et al. 2023), hindering researchers’ ability to understand the underlying economics. Firms may underreport security breaches because investors often react negatively to news of data breaches. For instance, Caesars Entertainment experienced a roughly 5% decrease in its stock price following the disclosure of a data breach and a subsequent $30 million ransom payment.4Additionally, firms must engage in costly reputation repair postbreach (Akey et al. 2024). The first step in protecting consumers, patients, investors, and businesses is to scientifically analyze the nature of the problem. That is the main goal of our study. Our study builds on the emerging literature on blockchain analytics—the science that exploits blockchain transparency to learn about the economic behavior of market participants (e.g.,Foley et al. 2019,Amiram et al. 2022,Makarov and Schoar 2022). We investigate the underlying economics of ransomware attacks and then broaden our analyses into a general discussion of crypto-enabled cybercrimes. Hence, we contribute to understanding the economics of organized crypto cybercrime, offering timely insights into crypto policy and regulation, laying the groundwork for future research. Information about crypto-enabled cybercrimes is typically dispersed, private, and incomplete. We assemble comprehensive on- and off-chain data sets from public (leaked) and proprietary data sources and augment them with manual information collection and data processing. The data sets include thousands of crypto-crime reports obtained from bitcoinabuse.com, aggregated scam reports obtained from chainabuse.com, transcripts of hundreds of negotiations between victims and attackers provided by a security breach management firm, thousands of hand-collected Bitcoin addresses used by cybercriminals, chat conversations between cybercriminals leaked by insiders or extracted by cybersecurity experts, and information on hundreds of attacks on U.S. and international corporations gathered directly from ransomware gangs on the dark web.5 These data allow us to quantify crypto-enabled cybercrimes and provide economic insights in the following manner. First, we conduct an in-depth exploration of the economics of ransomware, the most prominent form of crypto-enabled cybercrime.6Our goal is to understand the operations of dominant ransomware gangs, including their revenue models, extortion tactics, and reputation management strategies and the geographical locations of their victims. Next, we provide an overview and taxonomy of crypto cybercrime by evaluating thousands of scam reports and on-chain cybercrime activities. Finally, we identify key areas of interest for future research within the rapidly expanding crypto cybersecurity space. Our analysis and insights are mostly descriptive. As such, we do not claim a direct causal relationship between the emergence of cryptocurrencies and criminal activity. Instead, we leverage the transparency of permissionless blockchains to understand the economics of participants involved in crypto-related cybercrimes, thereby paving the way for future research in this field, providing initial insights to market participants and regulators. Our study contributes to the literature in at least three ways. First, the literature on crypto-based cybercrime, especially on ransomware, focuses on developing tools to detect ransomware attacks (e.g.,Alqahtani and Sheldon 2022,Kapoor et al. 2022), analyzing the consequences for enterprises (e.g.,Gopinath and Olmsted 2022,Palanisamy et al. 2022), and tracking ransom payments (e.g.,Conti et al. 2018,Huang et al. 2018). Our study is related toPaquet-Clouston et al. (2018), who estimate the financial impact of 35 ransomware groups from 2013 to 2017. In addition to providing more timely and broader economic evidence of ransomware activity, we differ from these studies by exploring unique off-chain data to reveal novel insights into the mechanics of ransomware attacks from infiltration techniques and negotiations to reputation management and affiliation strategy. Second, we provide a taxonomy of crypto-related cybercrimes across several blockchains. We complement recent literature focusing on a single blockchain, such as Bitcoin (Makarov and Schoar 2022) and Ethereum (Hornuf et al. 2023). Establishing a common taxonomy is the first step toward understanding the impact of cybercriminal activities, facilitating research, and improving communication clarity. Besides providing the scope of crypto-cybercrime and highlighting the large influence of blackmail scams, we also discuss the emergence of new types of cybercrimes, spurring more research in this space (e.g.,Griffin and Mei 2024). Finally, we join studies providing economic guidance for crypto regulation (e.g.,Cong et al. 2023b,c,2024;Rabetti 2023;Amiram et al. 2024;Campello et al. 2024). We complement these analyses by examining the landscape and organizational economics of crypto-enabled cybercrime, providing initial insights into the sophistication of cybercrimes and the efficacy of forensic finance in curbing these activities.7
article.2. The Economics of Ransomware: 2.1. Mechanics of Ransomware Attack The COVID-19 pandemic likely exacerbated the ransomware attacks as organizations shifted to remote work arrangements, rendering their cyber defenses more vulnerable (Cong et al. 2023a). Seizing upon these vulnerabilities, cybercriminals launched a wave of ransomware attacks. To carry out successful attacks, ransomware groups typically follow several steps. First, they gain access to target systems through various means, such as phishing emails, infected websites, stolen credentials, and the exploitation of software vulnerabilities. Once inside, the ransomware replaces the victim’s files with encrypted versions, often attempting to delete backups and shadow copies. Finally, the ransomware presents a ransom demand through altered display backgrounds, text files, or a customized dark website accessed via the Tor browser.8 Negotiations may occur, especially when large corporations or substantial ransoms are involved. To assist victims of security breaches, cybersecurity firms have emerged, offering services that include direct negotiation with ransomware gangs and managing cryptocurrency acquisition and payments on behalf of the victim. These consultants may negotiate with the attacker to try to reduce the ransom amount or just to stall so the victim has time to restore its files from backups and harden its security. Generally, ransomware attacks involve encrypting victims’ data and demanding payments in exchange for the decryption keys. However, since 2019, ransomware gangs have adopted an additional tactic, threatening to publicize the hacked data on the dark web. Recently, ransomware gangs have introduced a triple extortion strategy, threatening to send the victim’s hacked data directly to various stakeholders, including stockholders, business partners, employees, and customers. This new tactic underscores the growing sophistication of ransomware gangs’ extortion techniques. Consequently, coerced victims tend to comply with the demands, and payments, exclusively in crypto, occur within a few days of the attack. 2.2. Main Ransomware Gangs We assess leaked data from victims who refused to pay, obtained directly from postings by ransomware gangs on the dark web, to develop an overall view of the main players in this space.9These data contain 2,690 attacks carried out by 43 unique ransomware gangs.Figure 1shows the heat map for the distribution of victims per gang over time. We identified the most active ransomware gangs from May 2019 to July 2021: Conti, Sodinokibi (also known as REvil), and Maze.10 Ransomware groups frequently demand cryptocurrency payments with an increasing preference for more anonymous options, such as Monero, to avoid detection. Bitcoin payments may result in a premium fee imposed by the ransomware gang. It is important to note that successfully recovering the encrypted files without paying the ransom and receiving the decryption fee is a formidable challenge. The evolving nature of ransomware presents substantial cybersecurity challenges, underscoring the need for ongoing research and countermeasures to mitigate its impact on individuals, organizations, and society as a whole. 2.3. Negotiation We next examine hundreds of ransomware negotiations between attackers and victims to gain additional insights; see the example negotiation below.11First, it becomes evident that negotiating with these criminals presents significant challenges because they possess detailed knowledge about the financial well-being of their targets and other critical information, including whether they have cybersecurity insurance, which they often exploit when setting the ransom amount demanded.12 Second, although the attacker seeks to maximize profit, in our large sample of negotiations, victims have, on average, successfully negotiated discounts. Remarkably, in two thirds of the cases examined, these discounts exceeded 50%. Finally, we also learn that time is critical for both the victim and the attacker. The attacker aims to swiftly secure the demanded ransom amount, whereas the victim requires time to assess the breach’s scope and the sensitivity of the compromised data. Notably, the victim needs time to convert fiat into cryptocurrency, and the attacker is aware of this, potentially opening an additional venue for negotiations. However, the attacker often pressures the victim to expedite payment by threatening to leak documents or increase the ransom: –victim: “We thought we have almost 6 days left. Our leadership is currently reviewing the situation and determining the best resolution.” –attacker: “Until we waiting for your reply on situation. We stopped DDoS attack to your domain, you can switch on your website. As well your blog, where hidden. Nobody will see information about that, until we will not get in deal. We stopped already other instruments which already where processed today.” –victim: “Okay, thank you. We want to cooperate with you. We just need some time during this difficult situation.” –victim: “Can you please tell us what we will receive once payment is made?” –attacker: “You will get: 1) full decrypt of your systems and files 2) full file tree 3) we will delete files which we taken from you 4) audit of your network” –victim: “This situation is very difficult for us, and we are worried we may get attacked again or pay and you will still post our data. What assurances or proof of file deletion can you give us?” –attacker: “We have reputation and word, we worry about our reputation as well. After successful deal you will get: 1) full file trees of your files 2) after you will confirm we will delete all information and send you as proof video, we are not interested in to give to someone other your own data. We never work like that.” (Negotiation excerpt courtesy of Fox-IT NCC Group.) According to a security breach management expert at Fox-IT, payments are often made within a few days of the attack and primarily occur in cryptocurrency. The initial ransom demand is often accompanied by a time constraint with the price increasing if the demand is not met within a specified period. Failure to promptly engage in negotiations may result in additional tactics, such as denial of service attacks or releasing a small excerpt of controlled data on the dark web. 2.4. Revenue Models We first examine the indirect revenue model used by ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) platforms, on which a primary service provider rents out ransomware packages to affiliates. These platforms operate on a revenue-sharing basis, automatically splitting the ransom payments between the primary service provider and the affiliates. Table 1reports the compensation scheme based on the range of ransom payments.13The profitability of the RaaS model has led to its widespread adoption, and certain platforms, such as DarkSide ransomware, have implemented a tiered revenue split based on the ransom payment amount. The gang offers affiliates malware software and support services in exchange for a fee based on the revenue size or a fixed fee determined in advance. Blockchain forensics analysis inFigure 2reveals the flow of funds to the DarkSide gang, which receives a large share of the ransom payments, whereas the remaining 90% is split among affiliates and other intermediaries. Table 1.DarkSide Revenue Splits Collected from Dark Web Forums from 2019 to 2022 Table 1.DarkSide Revenue Splits Collected from Dark Web Forums from 2019 to 2022 Moving beyond the RaaS model, we explore the direct revenue model employed by ransomware gangs that prefer in-house operations and hire their own personnel. We analyze the operations of an unidentified ransomware gang (URG) and its subsidiary over two years. The subsidiary operated under the URG umbrella and generated approximately $45 million in ransom payments. Our analysis shows that around 17% of the victims chose to pay the ransom with the subsidiary experiencing high activity in June and July 2019. Examining the URG umbrella organization, we find that its revenue surpasses that of the subsidiary, reaching $161.8 million from 2018 to 2020. Like the subsidiary, URG experienced a ramp-up period before scaling its operations. The insights from this analysis suggest that cybercriminal activities do not face significant entry barriers, allowing for rapid expansion after establishing an initial revenue stream and operations. 2.5. Reputation Management Ransomware gangs seek to position ransomware attacks as regular business transactions rather than criminal activities and prioritize maintaining their public image. The gangs also engage in an ex ante analysis of victims’ profitability to determine the optimal ransom payment, considering factors such as the victim’s internal financial statements and cybersecurity insurance coverage. Each ransomware gang appears to have its own pricing formula and target preferences. For instance, a gang based in China uses the number of computers the victim possesses to estimate the size of the ransom payment as shown inTable 2.14 Table 2.Pricing Estimates Based on the Target’s Assets Table 2.Pricing Estimates Based on the Target’s Assets Source.Fox-IT NCC Group. Despite an increase in the aggressiveness of extortion games, ransomware gangs appear to value their reputation and public image as seen in an apology for a member attack on SickKids Hospital, a leading teaching and research hospital in Toronto focused on pediatric healthcare.15 In addition, the gang’s reputation depends not only on fulfilling its promises, such as decrypting data and deleting copied files, but also on an ex ante analysis of victims’ profitability to determine the optimal ransom payment size. Furthermore, ransomware gangs face reputation damage, particularly when their actions draw significant media attention. For instance, the gang responsible for the Colonial Pipeline attack came under intense scrutiny from U.S. government agencies, prompting the gang to rebrand itself—a tactic ransomware groups often employ as a means of vanishing from law enforcement radar before resuming operations under a different identity.16 2.6. Business-Like Operations To gain deeper insights into the modus operandi of a major ransomware group, we analyze Conti’s data, which include leaked chat logs and addresses.17The data encompass 168,661 chat logs and 448 unique handles (account names) from June 2020 to March 2022.Figure 3shows an example of the leaked chats.18 Working with cybersecurity experts and Russian translators, we analyzed thousands of leaked chats in Russian.19The chats reveal that Conti operates with a corporate mindset as evidenced by how it manages infrastructure expenses and pays employee salaries through a specific Bitcoin wallet address. Connections to previous ransomware payments made by the precursor gang Ryuk further confirm the evolution of Conti’s operations, likely rooted in rebranding strategies. The leaked data also contain chat logs from Conti’s leader, a handle called “Stern.” These messages provide insights into the gang’s plans, particularly its interest in using blockchain-based applications to launder ransomware proceeds. The discussions suggest a shift toward decentralized finance applications, possibly motivated by the growing demand for such services and the difficulties of converting crypto revenue to cash. 2.7. Geographic Preferences Little is known about where these gangs are located or their geographic preferences. Moreover, targets often underreport security breaches, obfuscating their identity, characteristics, and disclosure behavior. Therefore, we utilize dark web forums and cybersecurity experts’ infiltration strategies to extract attack information directly from ransomware gangs to provide insights into the geographic preferences of these gangs. The United States has the largest number of corporate victims of ransomware as seen inFigure 4.20Iran and North Korea, known for their links with local ransomware gangs, do not appear to be attacked. Of the gangs with more than 100 attacks worldwide, Conti (63%), Maze (62.9%), DoppelPaymer (55.4%), and Sodinokibi (53%) mainly target businesses headquartered in the United States; all these gangs are associated with Russia or Russian hackers according to their own declarations or investigations of their backgrounds.21Whether economic or political reasons drive attacks on U.S. corporations may be the subject of future research. Mounting evidence that Moscow’s tallest skyscraper openly hosts hackers, cybercriminals, and money launderers, for example, is consistent with cyberwarfare posturing.22
article.3. Crypto-Enabled Cybercrimes: Beyond their overall usage in payments, decentralized finance, and investments, cryptocurrencies are also attractive for illicit activities because of their technical ability to transcend national borders and unique jurisdictional issues, maintaining user anonymity (Trautman 2014,Gohwong 2019). Cryptocurrencies have been utilized in dark web markets not only for the sale of drugs (Foley et al. 2019), but also for trafficking chemical materials and even weapons (Broadhurst et al. 2020). Additionally, these digital currencies have facilitated money laundering (Wagman 2022) and terrorist financing (Amiram et al. 2022). Despite ongoing efforts to mitigate crypto cybercrimes, existing regulations remain inadequate in addressing the significant technological advancements and emerging business models within the cryptocurrency sector (Covolo 2019,Lyandres et al. 2022,Beale et al. 2023,De Simone et al. 2024). To highlight the scope and diversity of these illicit activities, it becomes essential to broaden our discussion from ransomware to encompass this wider range of crypto-related cybercrimes, starting with the dominant cryptocurrencies used in cybercrimes. 3.1. Blockchains and Bitcoin’s Dominant Role in Cybercrimes As the largest blockchain, Bitcoin naturally stands out with the most reported cybercrimes. Bitcoin accounts for 89% of reported scams, followed by Ethereum at 10%, according to chainabuse.com.23All other blockchains collectively contribute to less than 1% of reported cybercrimes. With the increasing sophistication of blockchain forensics and governmental agencies’ focus on combating crypto cybercrime, cybercriminals’ continued use of Bitcoin demands further investigation.24We hypothesize that, whereas Bitcoin is relatively traceable, cybercriminals believe the cryptocurrency’s convenience and the concomitant greater likelihood of receiving funds outweigh concerns about the increased chances of being caught by the authorities. 3.2. Taxonomy Establishing a common taxonomy is crucial to understanding the impact of various cybercriminal activities and to help facilitate research and enhance clarity and communication.25Based on the combination of chainabuse.com, bitcoinabuse.com, and terms from crypto-crime reports from leading forensic firms, such as Chainalysis and Elliptic, we construct a transparent taxonomy inFigure 5that provides a structured framework for categorizing various types of cybercrimes, offering researchers, law enforcement, and cybersecurity professionals a common language to discuss and analyze these issues. Broadly speaking, the crypto-related cybercrimes can be grouped into three main categories. The most substantial category, blackmail, constitutes approximately three quarters of the total number of reports.26In a blackmail scam, the perpetrator seeks payment from the target either to prevent the disclosure of purportedly damaging information or to unblock a vital service. The specific nature of these scams varies based on the type of information attackers use to manipulate their victims. Blackmail scams can be further classified into sextortion, ransomware, and other variations. Sextortion occurs when the scammer falsely claims possession of sensitive information, threatening to publicize it unless a cryptocurrency payment is made. In a ransomware scam, the perpetrator encrypts a computer system and demands payment for its release. Another variant of blackmail involves the threat of targeting the victim or its systems unless it transfers cryptocurrency funds. The second largest category, frauds, accounts for 11.5% of reported crimes. Fraud scammers employ a diverse range of tactics but tend to utilize several deceptive methods in pursuit of two main goals: first, to induce the victim to provide personal information, such as login details, which is then exploited for signing transactions and transferring funds on the victim’s behalf. Second, attackers may persuade victims to transfer crypto funds directly, often by assuming false identities, promising unreal returns, or associating themselves with fictitious projects. The methods used in such strategies also vary. Website phishing scams deploy counterfeit websites that mimic legitimate crypto projects or exchanges to trick users into revealing personal information. In another type of fraud, nonfungible token (NFT)/token airdrop, scammers send fake NFTs/tokens in bulk to users, hoping to lure them into phishing websites. Victims are then tricked into providing login information with which the attackers gain control and execute transactions. Fraudsters may also impersonate someone, usually celebrities, investors, nongovernmental organization (NGO) representatives, or project associates, with the aim of convincing targets to invest or donate directly or, in the case of an NGO or other such organization, to deceive them into donating crypto funds.27 Finally, there are investment scams. In the fake project variety, perpetrators masquerade as developers promoting nonexistent projects to solicit investments. In rug pull scams, attackers vanish after accepting funds from investors, leaving them without recourse. In another variant, fake returns, the attackers make false promises of returns by employing payout scams, Ponzi schemes, and pump-and-dump scams, among other tactics. The last of the largest categories, hacks, accounts for less than 1% of the total scam reports. Hacks encompass a variety of illicit activities in which hackers exploit vulnerabilities in smart contracts, protocols, infrastructure, or software to gain unauthorized access and manipulate funds or information. In Sim Swaps, hackers seize control of the victim’s cellphone to execute transactions on the victim’s behalf, leading to unauthorized fund transfers. Contract exploits leverage weaknesses in smart contracts to pilfer funds from wallets. Other hacking methods include protocol attacks that exploit vulnerabilities in governance and other protocols to steal funds. Man-in-the-middle or infrastructure attacks occur when scammers exploit communication network vulnerabilities to intercept information, accessing login details or private wallet keys. For instance, in domain name system (DNS) hijacking, hackers manipulate DNS queries to redirect users to malicious sites. These diverse hacking techniques underscore the multifaceted nature of cyberthreats in the crypto space. 3.3. Scope of Crypto-Enabled Cybercrimes As evidenced by thousands of crypto cybercrime reports across major blockchains, blackmail scams stand out as the most prevalent form of cybercrime. Given that most of these incidents occur within the Bitcoin network, we complement our initial assessment by analyzing on-chain payments on thousands of reports on the Bitcoin Abuse platform—originally hosted at bitcoinabuse.com but recently merged with chainabuse.com—to assess the scope of these scams. Our focus lies particularly on on-chain transactions associated with the primary types of blackmail scams to understand the most impactful cybercrime trends. The extracted information encompasses such details as the type of abuse, the reported address, and the number of reports. Additionally, we examine the number of transactions and the total amount received for each reported address. Table 3presents some summary statistics. On average, Bitcoin Abuse registers around 5,000 cybercrime reports per month. The reported cybercrimes are categorized into six groups: tumbler, blackmail scams, darknet market, ransomware, sextortion, and other. Consistent with our overview on scam reports by chainabuse.com, we find that sextortion reports have the highest count (33.8%), followed by blackmail (32.3%) and ransomware (23.9%). These three categories account for 94.4% ($157 million) of all reported entries on the Bitcoin blockchain. When considering the number of transactions, ransomware is the most active cybercrime on the Bitcoin blockchain, comprising 42.5% of the activity. Although not considered a cybercrime, Bitcoin tumblers account for 32% of the on-chain activity, and the remaining 22.4% falls under the “other” category, which includes phishing and impersonation scams and other frauds. Table 3.Reported Bitcoin Addresses Linked to Cybercrime Table 3.Reported Bitcoin Addresses Linked to Cybercrime Note.Computed from Bitcoin Abuse in 2022. Ransomware also leads in terms of total Bitcoin received, representing 47.1% ($156 million) of payments; not all Bitcoin received is directly related to payments as a significant portion is likely involved in reshuffling activities by cybercriminals, making it difficult to distinguish between the two. Our estimates suggest that ransomware is the most significant organized crypto-enabled cybercrime although other types of crimes using crypto, such as initial coin offering exit scams and rug pulls, generate billions in losses (e.g.,Cong et al. 2023a); these scams are not driven by organized criminal organizations.28
article.4. Conclusion and Future Research: Cryptocurrencies and decentralized finance are promising new technologies, but they also pose new risks and are prone to potential abuse, especially given how the anonymity of wallet ownership facilitates cybercrime. Our study provides the first detailed anatomy of crypto-enabled cybercrimes, exploring their scale, patterns, economic aspects, and tactics, such as revenue models, reputation management, and negotiation strategies. Therefore, it serves as a primer for future research on crypto-related cybercrime and provides insights relevant to policymaking.29 With crypto-related cybercrime on the rise, regulators across the globe are tempted to undertake extreme measures, such as instituting a blanket ban on cryptocurrency usage. Such a path is problematic for several reasons. First, blockchains typically operate globally, making regulations in one jurisdiction less effective elsewhere. Additionally, a ban may simply shift activities back to physical cash, which remains truly anonymous (although it may also expose victims to physical threats). Moreover, a ban may reduce technological innovation and international competitiveness (Harvey and Rabetti 2024). Our analysis suggests that the transparency and immutability of blockchain transactions can be leveraged to track, monitor, and reduce the probability of illicit activities. Using forensic finance, researchers can study the extent, nature, and geography of illicit transactions. 4.1. Future Research Recognizing the crucial role of data collection and processing in extracting insights from blockchain activity, researchers can develop sophisticated and powerful analytic tools for blockchain forensics. Such endeavors can help quantify and monitor cybercrimes and help reduce their probability, assessing the efficacy of various regulatory policies having implications in several areas. First, whereas we focus on the Bitcoin blockchain, alternative blockchains may exhibit distinct features and functionalities that enable different types of cybercrimes and scams. For example, data from chainabuse.com indicates a prevalence of phishing attacks on Ethereum (96.8%) over Bitcoin (2.1%).30Equally worthy of the attention of researchers, more opaque cryptocurrencies, such as Monero and Z-Cash, are increasingly being used in the cybercrime space. Therefore, future research should also explore the pervasiveness of cybercrime in non-Bitcoin blockchains. Second, although our study focuses on the most prominent type of crypto-related cybercrime, ransomware attacks, the landscape for cybercriminals is rapidly evolving, and several other types of crimes are occurring. For instance, a recent study byGriffin and Mei (2024)examines the economics of pig butchering, in which attackers receive economic payments from their victims, typically through a romance scam that preys on individuals experiencing depression. We believe that studies exploring more specific types of cybercrime represent promising avenues for future research as they may not only reveal new criminal networks, but also offer insights on how to mitigate these dark activities. Third, blockchain forensics can also reveal the mechanics of tax evasion. For instance,Cong et al. (2023b)find that investors respond to increased tax scrutiny by engaging in tax-loss harvesting as an alternative to noncompliance. Exploiting on-chain transfers,De Simone et al. (2024)documents that traders avoid taxation by pledging their assets as collateral in DeFi lending platforms. Research in the taxation space may also inform policymakers, that is, regarding upcoming regulation (e.g.,Beale et al. 2023). Finally, our study suggests that underreporting cybercrimes by U.S. firms is a pervasive phenomenon; research should seek to understand the motivations and assess the consequences behind such practices. Similarly, researchers may also examine the adverse effects of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) cybersecurity disclosure rules. For instance, the ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware gang has taken extortion to a new level by filing an SEC complaint against Meridian Link for not complying with the four-day rule to disclose a cyberattack.31
